Considering infinitesimally small displacement in the upper edge, the angular displacement $\theta$ of the rod axis from its original position would be : (shear moduli, $\left.\mathrm{G}=10^{10} \mathrm{~N} / \mathrm{m}^2\right)$

subject: Physics
q: Young's modules of material of a wire of length <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>L</mi></math> and cross-sectional area <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Y</mi><mo>.</mo></math> If the length of the wire is doubled and cross-sectional area is halved then Young's modules will be:
question: Young's modules of material of a wire of length <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>L</mi></math> and cross-sectional area <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Y</mi><mo>.</mo></math> If the length of the wire is doubled and cross-sectional area is halved then Young's modules will be:
solution: Young’s modulus depends on the material not length and cross-sectional area. So Young’s modulus will remain same irrespective of the dimension of the wire.

subject: Physics
q:
The area of cross section of the rope used to lift a load by a crane is 2.5×10-4 m2. The maximum lifting capacity of the crane is 10 metric tons. To increase the lifting capacity of the crane to 25 metric tons, the required area of cross section of the rope should be
(take g=10 ms-2)
question:
The area of cross section of the rope used to lift a load by a crane is 2.5×10-4 m2. The maximum lifting capacity of the crane is 10 metric tons. To increase the lifting capacity of the crane to 25 metric tons, the required area of cross section of the rope should be
(take g=10 ms-2)
solution:
Since breaking stress (Maximum lifting capacity) is the property of material, so it will remain same.

Now, breaking stress= Maximum lifting capacity  Area of cross section of rope 

⇒102.5×10-4=25 A

A=625×10-6

=6.25×10-4 m2

subject: Physics
q: Under the same load, wire A having length <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>0</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> and cross section <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>5</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></math> stretches uniformly by the same amount as another wire <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math> of length <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>0</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> and a cross section of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>5</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></math> stretches. The ratio of the Young's modulus of wire <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> to that of wire <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math> will be:
question: Under the same load, wire A having length <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>0</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> and cross section <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>5</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></math> stretches uniformly by the same amount as another wire <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math> of length <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>0</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> and a cross section of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>5</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></math> stretches. The ratio of the Young's modulus of wire <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> to that of wire <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math> will be:
solution:
Elongation in uniform wire under load is given by, Δl=FlSY, where S is area of cross-section.

As F is same for both wire and Δl is also same, we can write

ΔlF=lSY⇒lASAYA=lBSBYB

⇒52.5×10-5×YA=63×10-5×YB

⇒YAYB=1

subject: Physics
q: A string of area of cross-section <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo> </mo><msup><mi>mm</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></math> and length <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn></math> is connected with a rigid body of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math>. The body is rotated in a vertical circular path of radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math>. The body acquires a speed of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> at the bottom of the circular path. Strain produced in the string when the body is at the bottom of the circle is _____ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>5</mn></mrow></msup></math>. (Use Young's modulus <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>11</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>g</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math>)
question: A string of area of cross-section <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo> </mo><msup><mi>mm</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></math> and length <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn></math> is connected with a rigid body of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math>. The body is rotated in a vertical circular path of radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math>. The body acquires a speed of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> at the bottom of the circular path. Strain produced in the string when the body is at the bottom of the circle is _____ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>5</mn></mrow></msup></math>. (Use Young's modulus <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>11</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>g</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math>)
solution:
Given here, A=4 mm2, l=0.5 m, m=2 kg and vb=5 m s-1.

Tension at the bottom of the circular motion is given by T=mg+mvb2R

Using Young's modulus, we have 

Strain produced in the string when the body is at the bottom of the circle is ∆ll=TAY

=mg+mvb2lAY =20+2520.53×10-6×1011=30×10-5

subject: Physics
q: Two metallic wires <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Q</mi></math> have same volume and are made up of same material. If their area of cross sections are in the ratio <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo>:</mo><mo> </mo><mn>1</mn></math> and force <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>F</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> is applied to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi></math>, an extension of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>∆</mo><mi>l</mi></math> is produced. The force which is required to produce same extension in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">Q</mi></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>F</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math>. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><msub><mi>F</mi><mn>1</mn></msub><mrow><mo> </mo><msub><mi>F</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></mrow></mfrac></math> is ______.
question: Two metallic wires <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Q</mi></math> have same volume and are made up of same material. If their area of cross sections are in the ratio <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo>:</mo><mo> </mo><mn>1</mn></math> and force <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>F</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> is applied to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi></math>, an extension of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>∆</mo><mi>l</mi></math> is produced. The force which is required to produce same extension in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">Q</mi></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>F</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math>. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><msub><mi>F</mi><mn>1</mn></msub><mrow><mo> </mo><msub><mi>F</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></mrow></mfrac></math> is ______.
solution:
The formula for Young's modulus is given by

Y=FA∆ll=FlA∆l

From above equation, it follows that

Δl=FlAY   ...1

Again, volume of the wire is given by

V=Al⇒l=VA

Hence, from equation (1),

∆l=FVA2Y   ...2

As, Y and V is the same for both the wires,

∆l∝F A2

Hence, for two wires, it can be written that

∆l1∆l2=F1 A12×A22 F2   ...3

As ∆l1=∆l2, from equation (3), it follows that

F1 A22=F2 A12⇒F1 F2=A12 A22=412=16

subject: Physics
q: The strain-stress plot for materials $A, B, C$ and $D$ is shown in the figure. Which material has the largest Young's modulus ?
question: The strain-stress plot for materials $A, B, C$ and $D$ is shown in the figure. Which material has the largest Young's modulus ?
solution:
Young's modulus ($Y$) is defined as the ratio of stress to strain: $Y = \frac{\text{stress}}{\text{strain}}$.
In the given graph, the y-axis represents strain and the x-axis represents stress.
The slope of the line in a strain-stress graph is given by $m = \frac{\text{strain}}{\text{stress}}$.
Therefore, $Y = \frac{1}{\text{slope}}$.
To have the largest Young's modulus, the material must have the smallest slope in the strain-stress plot.
From the figure, line $C$ has the smallest angle with the stress axis (x-axis), meaning it has the minimum slope.
Since $Y \propto \frac{1}{\text{slope}}$, material $C$ has the largest Young's modulus.

subject: Physics
q: In an experiment to determine the Young's modulus, steel wires of five different lengths (1,2,3,4 and 5) but of same cross-section 2 mm2 were taken and curves between extension and load were obtained. The slope (extension/load) of the curves were plotted with the wire length and the following graph is obtained. If the Young's modulus of given steel wires is x×1011N m-2, then the value of x is _____ .
question: In an experiment to determine the Young's modulus, steel wires of five different lengths (1,2,3,4 and 5) but of same cross-section 2 mm2 were taken and curves between extension and load were obtained. The slope (extension/load) of the curves were plotted with the wire length and the following graph is obtained. If the Young's modulus of given steel wires is x×1011N m-2, then the value of x is _____ .
solution:
We know that, Young's modulus is Y=FLA∆L, where, F is force or load acting on wire, A is cross-section area, L is length and ∆L is extension in length.

Now, from given graph, the slope of the line is  =ΔLWL=ΔLLW=1YA

⇒Y=1slopeA

Thus, Young's modulus of the steel wires is  Y=12×10-60.25×10-5

⇒Y=2×1011 N m-2.

Therefore, x=2.

q: A man grows into a giant such that his linear dimensions increase by a factor of <math><mn>9</mn><mo>.</mo></math> Assuming that his density remains same, the stress in the leg will change by a factor of:
question: A man grows into a giant such that his linear dimensions increase by a factor of <math><mn>9</mn><mo>.</mo></math> Assuming that his density remains same, the stress in the leg will change by a factor of:
solution:
Stress =FA=MgA

=ρVolume .gA

=ρ93x3 g92x2

=9 times
